source_title: Teaching Data Journalism: Insights for Indian Journalism Education
author: Kashyap, Bhaskaran

extracts.extract: The pedagogic strategy should convey the importance of audience centrality and ethics in data journalism practice. It argues that such an approach can, in effect, improve industry practices as well as the quality of journalism education in India.
Any mistakes in the earlier stages of the project planning would affect the validity of the stories later. The skills being imparted will be restricted by the nature of the project.
The use of multiple data sources in a data story indicates deeper comparative analysis and richer context.

Scholars argue that these tools function as external entities which shape up journalistic narratives in data journalism because the journalists are not exercising editorial authority for optimal storytelling with available data but what can be done for free.

According to Gray and Bounegru (2019), the progression of data journalism practice shall focus on critical data practice where the reflexive critique of the entities (such as datasets, tools data sources or data infrastructures) is as important as the craft of data journalism practice.

For critical data literacy, a pedagogic approach like interrogating data using open datasets is effective. In this approach, classroom activities using a raw dataset are introduced to students. They are urged to interview the dataset by asking critical questions and explore it to find those answers.

Critical interaction with select data stories from industry outlets where students explore them from a user perspective to critique the relevance of interactive choices and transparency practices (such as sharing methodology, scripts and datasets) could be effective in this regard.
Developing a workflow chart which ensures every visualization created has basic interactive options and transparency will be effective, especially in a project-based, team effort.

Data literacy shall not be limited to numerical and technical competencies to work with datasets but shall include understanding about the socio-technical infrastructures which generate and store data. Literacy about data infrastructures shall enable journalists to hold such structures socially accountable.
